data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/03/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Alphabet's AI Power Play Is Hiding In Plain Sight - And Investors Are Missing It
data.description:
Let’s not dance around it—Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOG)(TSX:GOOG:CA) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) been getting the cold shoulder lately. The stock has taken a near-20% dive over the last three months and while that kind of pullback in a mega-cap name might trigger alarm bells, the fundamentals tell a completely different story.

Revenue’s growing. Margins are solid. AI is being rolled out across its entire ecosystem. And yet the company is being priced like it’s stuck in neutral. That disconnect, to me, is where the opportunity lies.

This isn’t a stock that needs a turnaround. It’s a cash machine with over $125 billion in operating cash flow, sitting on a $95 billion cash pile with just $10.9 billion in long-term debt, and running a business model that touches nearly every connected human being on Earth. The key change? It’s now an AI company that’s already deploying products at a global scale—while the rest of the world is still talking about prototypes.

Where the Market's Missing the Plot

The sell-off doesn’t line up with reality. There’s no earnings miss. No regulatory ruling has dropped. No key part of the business is falling apart. So why’s the stock off its highs?

Most investors are busy watching Nvidia put up blockbuster numbers and listening to OpenAI tease its next model drop. Alphabet, by contrast, hasn’t delivered a flashy demo lately—but that’s the thing: it doesn’t need to. The company already has its AI systems—Gemini in particular—baked into everything from Search and Android to Gmail, Chrome and even YouTube. These aren’t pilot projects. They’re fully embedded.

Google Cloud used to be the underdog in cloud earnings calls. Not anymore. Revenue jumped 30.6% year-over-year, but the real story is operating income, which shot up from $1.7 billion to $6.1 billion. That kind of operating leverage doesn’t happen by accident.

Meanwhile, Search remains Alphabet’s fortress. With Gemini now powering “AI Overviews” users are getting more direct answers and behind the scenes, Alphabet is saying those AI summaries are actually lowering the cost to serve each query. That’s good for users and even better for margins.

What the 10-K Tells Us

A skim through Alphabet’s 10-K shows more than just standard accounting language. The tone is full of hype—it’s measured and focused. The company is investing heavily—$49.3 billion into R&D last year alone—but it’s not doing that to chase headlines. This is long-term positioning.

One detail that stands out: Alphabet has unified its AI development under the Gemini brand and Google DeepMind, streamlining both research and deployment. The result? Gemini is already driving features across Workspace (Docs, Gmail) helping small businesses create YouTube ads and powering on-device functionality in Pixel phones. And management’s not just talking about “transformative AI.” They’re actively redesigning the architecture of how Google Search itself works. That’s not trivial.

Also interesting—Alphabet’s approach to cookies. After years of pushing a total phase-out of third-party tracking, they backtracked and moved to a more nuanced user-choice model. That kind of pivot tells me they’re not just checking legal boxes—they’re actively responding to regulatory pressure with smarter product decisions.

Let’s Talk Numbers

On nearly every metric, Alphabet looks wildly undervalued:

  • Forward P/E: 17.4x

  • EV/EBITDA: 10.6x

  • Free Cash Flow Yield: ~4%

  • ROE: 32.9%

  • Operating Margin: 32%

  • Cash, Cash Equivalents: $95B+

These are numbers you typically see in slow-growth, mature businesses—except Alphabet isn’t one. Revenue rose nearly 14% last year to $350B. Cloud is growing at a 30% pace. YouTube keeps printing cash. And AI monetization is only just beginning.

What does that mean for valuation? Well, even a modest re-rating back to a 22x multiple (still below Alphabet’s five-year average of 25.4x) would push the stock closer to $180. And if Gemini gains traction across enterprise and consumer segments—as I believe it will—a 25–26x multiple on $9+ in EPS isn’t far-fetched. That puts upside targets closer to $210–$230+ per share.

What the Headlines Are Actually Saying

The narrative around Alphabet is shifting but slowly. Recent coverage has highlighted it as “the cheapest of the Magnificent Seven” pointing to increased interest from hedge funds. The tone has turned more upbeat, framing Alphabet as an under-the-radar AI contender rather than a worn-out tech giant.

OpenAI’s move to release an open-weight model also made the rounds but the market didn’t flinch. If anything, sentiment skewed mildly bullish for Alphabet because developers might get more flexibility—but Google’s strength isn’t in novelty. It’s in scale. Gemini is already in billions of hands across Gmail, Android and Search.

So while the media spotlight keeps chasing toward whatever the latest AI model is, Alphabet is busy winning the part of the game that actually pays: distribution, integration and repeatable monetization.

Risks Worth Taking Seriously

Alphabet isn’t short on strengths, but it’s not without pressure. The legal and regulatory heat has been simmering for a while, and it’s coming to a head in a few key places. For starters, the DOJ already landed a blow on Google Search with a ruling in late 2024 and the fallout—likely structural—is expected to hit later this year. That case alone could reshape how Google integrates Search with ads. There’s also a separate front on ad tech, with both federal and state lawsuits stacking up. Europe isn’t backing off either. Alphabet’s dealing with the consequences of the Digital Markets Act and multiple billion-euro fines tied to bundling practices and platform behavior.

That’s the legal side. Then there’s data and privacy—arguably more volatile because of how fast it’s evolving. With Gemini rolled out across Workspace, Android and Chrome, Google is now sitting on even more personal data than before. And that makes it a target not just for hackers but for regulators. The company’s recent decision to pivot on third-party cookie removal—essentially slowing down its own plan—signals that it’s feeling the pressure. Alphabet’s playing a high-stakes game here: innovate fast but stay on the right side of law and public perception.

AI brings another layer of complexity. It’s not just about staying competitive with OpenAI or Microsoft. It’s about ensuring the models don’t hallucinate, discriminate or get misused. One bad rollout, especially in Search or Cloud, could be a reputational mess—and Google knows it.

You also can’t ignore the operational stuff. Cybersecurity risks are growing as the company expands its AI and Cloud footprint. A data breach or outage in any one of its global data centers could have ripple effects. And then there’s global exposure—more than half of Alphabet’s revenue comes from outside the U.S. That means exchange rates, local laws, trade friction—all of it matters.

So yeah, the risks are layered and very real. But they’re not new. What’s changed is how intertwined they’ve become with AI data and distribution. It’s not about if there’s risk—it’s about whether the market’s already baking it in. At this price? It probably is.

Bear vs. Base vs. Bull

Bear Case ($130):If AI monetization stalls, regulators hit hard and Search loses share, earnings could drop and multiples shrink. Even then a 20x multiple on $6.50 EPS implies a floor in the low $130s.

Base Case ($180):More likely, earnings grow steadily to $8.25-8.5 and the stock trades back at 22x. That gives you solid upside without needing anything extraordinary.

Bull Case ($210–230+):If Gemini becomes the productivity layer across Cloud, Workspace, Android and Search and Cloud keeps scaling profits, EPS hits $9–9.25 and the stock re-rates to 25–26x. That’s 30–45% upside from here.

Final Take

This isn’t a trade for next week. It’s a long-term position for people who care about fundamentals, not headlines. Alphabet is turning AI from a research project into a revenue stream, and the market hasn’t caught up. You’re getting fat margins, industry-leading infrastructure and a bulletproof balance sheet—all at a discount.

No it’s not the most exciting name in the AI hype cycle right now. But it's the one that’s quietly integrating itself deeper into how we search, watch, navigate and work—and making a fortune doing it.

Bottom line: this is a strong buy and one of the best setups in big tech right now—if you’re willing to look past the noise.

data.title: Duolingo: Encouraging Trends Ease AI Concerns, Valuation Still Stretched
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) previously with a hold rating as I was very worried about the impact of AI on DUOL's business model. Fast forward to today, while my concerns haven’t completely gone away, I think I may have underestimated DUOL’s relevance. Recent data suggest the platform is still very relevant—and possibly more defensible than I initially thought. I don’t think this is sufficient to warrant an upgrade, but I believe there are enough positive developments to put this back on the watchlist for a potential buy rating upgrade at some point.

A refreshed view on DUOL

While only a little over two months have passed, new data has surfaced that made me reconsider my view on the DUOL business over the long term. Do not get me wrong, I am still very worried about the impact from AI, but I am also cognizant that I may have been too pessimistic about DUOL’s ability to navigate this situation. There are a couple of reasons/data points that led me to say this.

Firstly, the engagement trends highlight relevance. The latest numbers show that daily active users [DAU] reached 40.5 million, which represents ~51% y/y growth, while monthly active users [MAU] grew 32% y/y to 116.7 million. These are not performance metrics that suggest DUOL is losing demand. The biggest signal was beyond the headline metrics, but the engagement depth—DAU/MAU ratio—which rose ~430 bps y/y to 34.7%. This improvement suggests that DUOL is not only gaining more users, but it also has increasing daily relevance. This is huge because I would’ve thought that DUOL is losing relevance because of AI.

Notably, as I relooked at DUOL's business model, one attractive aspect is that most of the new users are acquired organically, according to management. This shows that DUOL continues to benefit from product virality, which further supports the fact that DUOL is still very relevant in the user’s daily life. The other interesting part is that DUOL could acquire more users if it wanted to by ramping up marketing dollars. This is a big growth lever that I thought was worth highlighting because it could significantly extend the growth runway should the pace of growth start to decelerate.

Perhaps the more important data point is that DUOL continues to add paid subscribers. In 4Q24, paid subscribers grew 43% y/y to 9.5 million and are only still less than 9% of the total MAU base. Here is the bull case: If DUOL continues to stay relevant, then it could be said that DUOL is still in its early innings of converting MAU to paid subscribers. And management is actively rolling out products like pricing tiers like Family Plan and Max to ramp up conversion. Take Max for instance; management already confirmed that Duolingo Max is now available to nearly all iOS users globally, and while Max currently accounts for only about 5% of paid subscribers, early adoption feedback among power users has been great.

Secondly, DUOL efforts in expanding outside of its core usage—language—have seen solid traction. A while back, DUOL built out new verticals like math and music, and management has noted that both DUOL Math and Music now have >3 million weekly active users, and the apps have expanded from just two languages at launch to over 10 today. Beyond the obvious benefit that these could become meaningful revenue contributors over time, they also serve as a hedge against the threat of general-purpose AI tools like ChatGPT or Gemini (which is my key concern). While language can be easily disrupted, math and music, on the other hand, aren’t as easily disrupted by these models. They require a more sophisticated model and user interface (more structure, more sequencing, and a lot more reinforcement), and I think this is where DUOL excels—in gamifying learning. So instead of being overly reliant on one product category (language), I was very encouraged to see the traction that DUOL has in building a broader learning platform that’s stickier, harder to replicate, and much more defensible over time.

Finally, there is very encouraging alternative data that add another layer of assurance to DUOL being relevant. Note that these data are from JPMorgan, and I credit them for this.

In a recent survey cited, 18% of users reported being able to read or write in another language, up from 11% in September. Meanwhile, 15% said they can hold a conversation, also up from 11%. This not only highlights product effectiveness but also shows that it is delivering value to consumers. Additionally, according to Sensor Tower, DUOL continues to see solid downloads growth.

Valuation

I think a multiple closer to 8x forward revenue would be a much better entry point. At 8x, even if DUOL were to print rapid decelerating growth (remember that DUOL still can ramp up marketing to capture growth), and multiple stays flat, there is upside to the stock.

In any case, as of today, I think the recent data and developments have certainly convinced me to a certain extent that DUOL will not be completely disrupted by AI. However, I am still not ready to upgrade my rating to a buy until I see a few more quarters of data that show DUOL not being impacted by the risk I noted previously. More importantly, as I mentioned above, I don’t think DUOL is cheap enough yet. The stock still trades at a premium to its historical average multiple of 10x forward revenue. There is not enough margin of safety in the event that the bear case (competitive threat from AI) materializes.

Conclusion

I still give a high rating to DUOL. I’m encouraged by the new data points around engagement depth, subscription traction, and DUOL’s success in adjacent learning categories. These are all signs that DUOL may be more resilient to AI disruption than I initially feared. That said, the valuation still doesn’t offer enough downside protection, and I’d prefer to see a few more quarters of data before getting more bullish.

data.title: KinderCare Learning: Poor Core Business Performance And Lingering Uncertainty (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to KinderCare Learning (NYSE:KLC) in November last year, with my key thesis being that KLC can capitalize on the underlying secular trend. That turned out to be a bad call, as the stock price has fallen sharply over the past few months. I no longer think that KLC is an attractive buy. The core business failed to live up to my expectations, and the significant shift in the political climate presents a major risk to a substantial portion of KLC revenue. My revised model shows that KLC is priced at where it should be, and as such, I downgrade KLC to hold.

Earnings results update

In the latest quarter (4Q24) reported about two weeks ago, KLC grew revenue by 4.7% y/y to $647 million, of which most of the growth was driven by pricing. Profitability was a bright spot. KLC delivered adj. EBITDA of $66 million, which was a ~5% y/y growth. Basically, in line with top-line growth (margin was flat at 10.2% margin). That said, on a GAAP basis, the results were less positive. KLC continues to report a GAAP net loss of $133.6 million in 4Q24, which was primarily driven by $123 million in stock-based compensation.

Overall, the headline figures were not bad, but they weren’t great either, as they tracked significantly below my model forecasts. I do find it encouraging that the Champions business (before & after school segment) continues to grow print double-digit growth (grew 12.5% y/y to $54 million in 4Q24, with site growth contributing 8%), which suggests that there is still demand headroom across the nation. The B2B segment also showed continued momentum. In FY24, KLC opened six new employer-sponsored centers and signed eleven new employer clients in 4Q24 alone. This momentum should continue as employers look for ways to support working parents and improve return-to-office compliance. So, even if enrollment recovery remains sluggish on the consumer side, these enterprise partnerships are great additions as they provide a stable source of demand.

Core business trends remain under pressure

However, the challenge is that the broader ECE (early childhood education) business still isn’t showing signs of meaningful acceleration. Occupancy fell again sequentially, coming in at 67.9% in 4Q24 compared to 68.6% in 3Q24. More importantly, management guided to relatively flat occupancy for FY25, which suggests KLC may be entering a period of stagnation. This is particularly disappointing given that KLC continues to open new centers. Without an uptick in enrollment per site (barely up 1% in 4Q24), the incremental return on new builds will not be great.

Revenue guidance also came in soft. For FY25, KLC guided for $2.75 billion to $2.85 billion in total revenue, which equates to just 5.1% y/y growth at the midpoint. That’s a full 300 bps below my original estimates, and the worrisome thing is that most of that guided growth is expected to come from price, not volume. I am not positive about this pricing-led growth strategy as the consumer spending environment is being strained by various other macro factors (persistent inflation, higher mortgage rates, and tariffs that could further fuel inflation).

Hence, my view has turned a lot less bullish. Without a clear path to reaccelerate growth via more volume, I think it will be difficult for the stock to rerate meaningfully higher from here.

Rising government funding risk adds a new layer of uncertainty

Aside from the pressure at the core business level, a major cause of concern is the growing risk to KLC subsidy exposure. I previously cited this as a very positive factor for KLC, but unfortunately, the situation has flipped. I now think this is a major overhang on the stock sentiment. As per management, ~35% of KLC’s total revenue is funded by government programs like the Child Care and Development Fund [CCDF]. And the risk is that a substantial portion of this revenue could go away because of the current US political climate. I am not an expert on politics; hence, my views are shaped by research and reports from renowned parties. In particular, according to recent reporting by the Center for American Progress and CLASP, proposed federal budget cuts—including those under the “Project 2025” framework—could significantly reduce access to key childcare subsidies. In fact, in the CLASP report, the estimate was that ~40,000 children are at risk. This comes at a time when certain states are already facing funding shortfalls. Which means some states actually need more funding, and if they don’t get it, thousands of children will continue to lose access to these assistance programs. Even if these cuts don’t materialize anytime soon, the uncertainty around long-term funding itself is a major overhang that will continue to limit the share price upside.

Valuation

To reflect the developments, I have adjusted my model accordingly and now believe that KLC is priced at where it should be. The core business failed to live up to my expectations, and management’s guidance confirms that we are not going to see any form of acceleration. The additional uncertainty revolving around the government funding is a major risk, in my opinion, that could really impact near-term earnings. For modeling's sake, I assumed the same mid-single-digit revenue growth and the same adj. EBITDA margin for FY25/26. With no revenue growth acceleration and the huge uncertainty overhang, I see very little chance for the market to re-rate KLC’s multiple. As such, I am downgrading my rating from buy to hold.

Conclusion

My downgrade to a hold rating is because I don’t think KLC is able to deliver the results that I expected. The core business has not seen any form of recovery/acceleration, and a sizeable portion of KLC business is under risk of disruption by the shift in the political climate. With a mid-single-digits revenue growth outlook, I don’t see this as an attractive business to invest in, and at the current valuation, I believe KLC is trading at where it should be.

data.title: Pollard Banknote: Getting Close To The Discount I Like
data.description:
Note: All monetary values in this article are expressed in Canadian dollars, unless otherwise explicitly stated.

More Than Just a Scratch-Off Game

Have you ever tried your luck with a Gold Rush scratch-off?

Who knows? Sometimes you might be just one scratch away from retirement—leaving all the mess of Trump tariffs and the tech bubble behind to kick back on a beach at some Paradise Island.

You might not know this, but the company behind Gold Rush is Pollard Banknote (TSX:PBL:CA)(OTCPK:PBKOF).

Exactly one hundred and nineteen years ago, what was then known as Saults & Pollard was just another commercial printing company in Winnipeg, Manitoba. After sixty years of general printing, Pollard began focusing on security printing—a wise move that defined what this Canadian company would become in the years to come.

But why is this such a big deal?

After years of working in general printing, producing simpler items like leaflets, packaging, or promotional materials, they were stuck with lower margins, tougher competition, and a low barrier to entry. To survive, they had to focus on volume, and they did so for more than half a century.

They moved on to printing banknotes, passports, government documents, and checks. With this came a specialized demand and a need to upgrade their technologies, like holograms, special inks, and microtext. The result was higher margins, a portfolio of important clients, and a more favorable market position.

This is the Mengerian process of specialization happening right before our eyes!

It was only a matter of time before they entered the lottery business.

Said and done.

In the mid-eighties they entered this market, launching the well-known Gold Rush in June 1988 in partnership with the British Columbia Lottery Corporation.

But it wasn’t just about the lottery. Pollard branched out into other segments through strategic acquisitions—much like companies such as IGT (IGT), Light & Wonder (LNW), and others in the same industry. The difference, however, is that they didn’t start with slot machines.

In fact, they operate in the following segments:

  1. Lotteries: They design, manufacture, and distribute a wide range of products, including instant tickets, ticket distribution systems, digital games, and iLottery software for lotteries around the world. As I mentioned before, they started with the Gold Rush, but today they’re much bigger than that;
  2. Charitable Gaming: Through its subsidiary Pollard Charitable Games Group, Pollard distributes and manufactures pull-tabs, bingo products, and innovative electronic gaming solutions. For those who may not recall, Pollard has been entering this market little by little. In 2018, they acquired International Gamco, an American manufacturer of charitable gaming products. They continue to make acquisitions to strengthen their portfolio, such as Compliant Gaming in 2020 (US$19 million), Clarence J. Venne in 2024 (US$12.6 million) and Pacific Gaming this year (US$10 million);
  3. iGaming: It's not just iLottery that Pollard operates. Eleven years ago, they entered into a joint venture with NeoGames, creating NeoPollard Interactive. They kicked off with lottery games for Michigan and Virginia, but after snapping up companies like Mkodo and Next Generation Lotteries, they've dipped their toes deeper into iGaming—think digital bingo and pull-tabs for charity, not just lotteries. Still, they're not going full-on casino or sports betting yet;
  4. Retail systems: Pollard offers a range of retail solutions to make life easier for its customers. These include ticket dispensers, such as EasyVend, and eye-catching point-of-sale displays in stores. Pollard also offers scanACTIV, which enables the sale of tickets directly at checkout or self-service kiosks, seamlessly integrating with existing payment systems. It didn’t grow from scratch here either. Acquisitions such as Fastrak Retail in 2019 have helped shape Pollard’s path.

In FY 2024, Pollard Banknote hit an all-time high in sales, thanks to a newly signed contract with Kansas iLottery and a five-year renewal (with an option for another five) to supply instant tickets for the Michigan Lottery, which makes up around 90% of Michigan’s lottery demand. Even though they’ll be replaced by another iLottery vendor in 2026, the question remains—does Pollard Banknote deserve a spot in your portfolio?

We know they know how to print tickets like nobody’s business.

To give you an idea, when they first started providing scratch-off tickets to the Michigan Lottery, instant ticket sales grew 20% from FY 2017 to FY 2019. If we compare that to the national growth of the instant ticket segment, they grew by 11% in the same period—almost double!

But can we take this for granted?

Of course not. IGT recently launched a proprietary printing system called POP that is generating significant buzz in Italy.

Let’s take a closer look at FY 2024 and see where we stand

iLottery Joint Venture Saves the Day

As I mentioned earlier, Pollard Banknote saw its FY 2024 sales reach an all-time high. They were $557.1 million, growing 7.05% year-over-year.

In the post-pandemic period, operational costs have increased significantly due to higher volumes of charitable gaming, sales of auxiliary products, and inefficiencies in the production of instant tickets. However, on an annual basis, the performance has been better than FY 2023.

This caused EBITDA to grow approximately 29.3% year-over-year to $42.8 million.

With SG&A flat year-over-year, EBIT soared 154% year-over-year to $18.3 million. If you look closely, you’ll notice that this is still below most pre-pandemic years and in line with the post-pandemic period except FY 2023.

Looking down, interest on debt remained at $10.3 million, down just $2 million year-over-year thanks to a $26.6 million refinancing of long-term debt in FY 2024.

Most importantly, the joint venture brought in $52.6 million for Pollard—up 34.52% from $39.1 million last year. This was offset by higher taxes, which resulted in a net income of $35.2 million, up 12.1% year-over-year.

This net income isn’t just impressive—it’s Pollard Banknote’s highest ever, thanks to its joint venture. This is also the highest normalized EPS in the last ten years, reaching $1.21.

Looking at the cash flow statement, there’s a curious picture here. Pollard’s operating cash flow has been on a steady upward trend, hitting $73.9 million—up 14.39% year-over-year. It would actually generate strong FCF if they didn’t make so many small acquisitions.

That is, it’s a classic lottery operator—steady and growing cash flows, strong OCF, low Capex, and lots and lots of acquisitions.

At the end of the day, they had invested $77.2 million in the business, which ended up with a deficit of at least $3.3 million. To cover this, Pollard leveraged its balance sheet a bit more by issuing $21 million of net debt. About $5.1 million was returned to shareholders, growing slightly year over year on a continuing basis.

This led to $16.8 million in financing inflows, resulting in a cash surplus of $13.9 million.

Pollard now holds $22.4 million in cash on hand. This may not seem like much, but it would be a great FCF generator if it weren't for strategic acquisitions. Since these acquisitions happen at opportune moments, the company can afford to keep fewer liquid assets.

But can Pollard keep making these acquisitions and leveraging itself up more and more? The answer is a bit complex. If we look at EBITDA alone, Pollard appears to have a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of close to 3.5x. But let’s not forget the iLottery joint venture. That alone added $52.6 million to net income, which is more than the EBITDA generated by the lottery operations.

So yes, Pollard has room to take on more debt if needed

We can sum it up this way:

  1. Pollard generates steady revenue from lottery sales and its long-term contracts with state lotteries. This should continue to drive mid-single-digit revenue growth. Importantly, they are making an effort to reprice instant tickets and this strategy will be fully realized in FY 2025. If sales volumes of these tickets remain intact despite price increases, I would expect high-single-digit year-over-year growth;
  2. iLottery appears to be the biggest growth catalyst here. Pollard recently entered into an agreement with Kansas iLottery and initial results are in line with expectations. Keep in mind, Michigan iLottery stays with Pollard through FY 2026, so FY 2025 could be full steam ahead;
  3. They could and should make acquisitions during FY 2025. But that really won't be a problem. The balance sheet looks healthy when we adjust for joint venture earnings, and dividends are a small part of that. The downside is that Pollard doesn't do buybacks;
  4. Pollard also said that the potential impacts of tariffs will be mitigated, as most inputs are purchased from local suppliers;
  5. Management said strong demand for Pollard's products and solutions, including instant packages, charitable games and iLottery, is expected to continue into FY 2025.

The sad part is that they didn’t provide clear quantitative guidelines.

We’ll have to rely on intuition, with a bit of creativity, to fill in the gaps!

Valuation: Are Pollard Shares Worth the Winning Ticket?

I always start with the good old Comps Model.

But today we're going to use the NPVGO Model first.

I will be using the following assumptions for my model:

  1. The analyst consensus estimate for FY 2025 EPS is $1.86. This would represent at least 43% year-over-year growth;
  2. The estimated dividend per share for FY 2025 is $0.25 per share. This would give us a payout ratio of 13%. Of course, the retention ratio will be 87%;
  3. Since Pollard does not do buybacks, I will be assuming an outstanding share count of 27.1 million. This would give us a net income of roughly $50.4 million and an implied ROA of approximately 7%. Note that this ROA is higher than the one reported in FY 2024 (5.53%) and lower than Pollard's 5-year average (8.09%), which is within the normal range here;
  4. Discount rate of 7.1%.

Based on these assumptions, the model provides us with the following insights:

  • With a 7% ROA, the IGR is 6.09% (7% x 87%). Since Pollard's retention per share is $1.62, the growth from retention in Year 1 is $0.11;
  • The NPV of holding is -$0.02 per share. Modeling this in perpetuity, we would have a perpetual NPVGO of -$2.26;
  • The value of Pollard's stock if it were a cash cow would be $26.20. That is, if it distributed all of its EPS and modeled those cash flows in perpetuity under the discount rate mentioned above, we would have a value per share of $26.20;
  • The value of the stock under the NPVGO Model would be the sum of these two assumptions. This would give us a PT of$23.94.

NPVGO indicates that, although the stock appears discounted, the potential upside is 19.2%, with a safety margin of approximately 16%.

It still doesn’t seem like a bargain to me, not like IGT—a major player in the lottery space—or even Betsson AB (OTCPK:BTSBF).

Now let's take a look at the multiples.

Starting with the good old P/E, with the analyst consensus EPS of $1.86, we would be looking at a projected P/E of 10.79x. This is about 26.5% below the industry median, 19.71% below IGT and up to 33% below other peers such as Inspired Entertainment (INSE). If it were trading at IGT's multiple, we would be looking at a PT of $24.99.

Note that this is very close to what we found at NPVGO.

Now assuming sales of $596.05 as per analyst estimates for FY 2025, we would have an RPS of approximately $21.99. This would be a P/S of approximately 0.91x. This is 10.97% higher than the industry median of 0.82x.

I will now be using EV multiples to consider capital structure.

Assuming an EBITDA margin of 7.7%, which is in line with Pollard's 5-year average and FY 2024, and the consensus for FY 2025 revenue, we would have an EBITDA of $45.89 million. With an EV of $682.1 million, the EV/EBITDA ratio would be 14.86x.

Is that low? It may be for other industries, but for gaming technology companies for lotteries and casinos the median is 9.36x. This implies that Pollard is 58% above the industry median.

In any case, I don't believe that EBITDA or even EBIT are that significant in our assessment.

They simply can't measure the impact of the joint venture here.

I'll stick with the P/E and the NPVGO Model. Both seem to tell us that Pollard is trading slightly below its fair value.

It's somewhere between $23 and $25, which might tell us that although discounted, it's not a bargain.

That said, I’d wait a bit longer—perhaps until the stock is in the high $10s—to pick it up at a better discount. After all, there are more affordable opportunities out there, particularly in the casino and lottery technology space.

My recommendation for Pollard is 'Hold', with a PT of $23 to $25.

Keep an eye on the next few days. If the stock dips into the high-teens, it could be a great time to buy, especially if it hits a market bottom around $18 or even $16. The latter would be an excellent entry point. But for those not chasing the biggest bargain, $18 is already a solid buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pacific Premier Bancorp: The Numbers Are On Its Side, But Keep An Eye On The Macroeconomic Factor (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In my last article on Pacific Premier Bancorp (NASDAQ:PPBI) I highlighted the soundness of this bank, but I didn't consider it a buy because it wasn't cheap enough. Since that day the stock has plummeted 17%, and today its Tangible Book Value is priced well below its historical average. In addition, the dividend yield has skyrocketed to over 6%.

With the introduction of tariffs, the macroeconomic context has worsened, but PPBI still seems a valid option given its conservative approach. Its capital ratios are excellent, as is its liquidity compared to total assets. Its high exposure to CRE loans is a negative factor, but historically PPBI has managed credit risk well.

High quality deposits and loans gradually recovering

The main strength of this bank lies in its deposits, which are mainly made by loyal customers who do not require high interest rates.

As you can see from this image, noninterest-bearing deposits amount to 32% of the total, which is a significant figure. Furthermore, 52% of the total deposits are made up of money market/savings and interest-bearing deposits, both with a rather low interest rate. The result is that total non-maturity deposits are 85% of the total and have an average cost of only 1.28%.

Overall, the average cost of total deposits amounted to 1.79% in Q4 2024, a much lower figure than the Fed Funds Rate: these are the kind of banks I like. When a bank manages to have such solid deposits, I am more confident about its recovery.

The problem with this bank lies in the low growth rate of loans and their respective yields.

In Q4 2024, the net interest margin fell by 14 basis points mainly due to lower loan yields, while deposits had a positive effect. Until loans start growing again, the NIM cannot improve, even if deposits have a very low cost.

Yet, this bank has ample liquidity at its disposal that it could invest to generate new loans.

The loan to deposit ratio is only 83%, so there is ample room to maneuver to improve the balance sheet. In 2024, there was a lack of demand for new loans, but in the last quarter a glimmer of hope emerged.

Loan production increased significantly (+$834 million), but not all of this can be attributed to organic growth. The activity of creating new loans has increased, with a total of $316 million in new loan commitments; the remaining part was purchased: $401.30 million in investment grade C&I loans and $116.30 million in single-family residential loans. In a sense, if PPBI cannot independently increase its loan portfolio, it makes sense for it to purchase new loans from other banks.

This strategy will also be pursued in the coming quarters, after all PPBI cannot remain with so much unused liquidity. However, all this must not overshadow organic growth, which is clearly recovering compared to previous quarters.

We saw the benefit from the strategic actions we took during the summer to positively impact loan production. We have improved our competitive position in the market, which has translated into a nice pickup in new loan activity that carried an attractive weighted average rate on new originations of 6.92%. The team is successfully managing existing portfolio balances ahead of scheduled loan maturities and interest rate resets. As a result, we saw $95 million in multifamily production for existing customers.

Having moved past key factors that previously fueled broader uncertainty, we anticipate expanding our loan production efforts consistent with our ability to attract low-cost deposits. In the coming quarters, we expect organic originations to meet or exceed the level of prepayments and payoffs.

Steve Gardner - Chairman & Chief Executive Officer

PPBI seems better positioned in the market than before, which gives me hope for its future. At the moment, management expects low- to mid-single-digit loan growth, nothing exceptional, but at least it's a starting point. Deposits will remain strong and low-cost, so there is no rush to improve the loan portfolio.

The latter has disappointed in terms of average yield, but in terms of risk, PPBI assets are outperforming their peers once again.

Non-performing assets have a negligible weight on total assets, and this figure has been constantly improving since the beginning of 2024.

Valuation and dividends

PPBI is not a fast-growing bank, but its deposits are of high quality and must be worth something.

Looking at the Price/Tangible Book Value of the last 10 years, the historical average is 1.90x, while currently this ratio is only 1.03x. It is almost half and personally, I don't think it is fair to undervalue this bank so much.

Surely EPS has suffered a blow in recent years, but Tangible Book Value per share has never stopped growing, and this is a crucial factor for a bank. If PPBI were traded at even a multiple of 1.40x, it would mean that it would be traded at $29.35 per share, an increase of 40% from the current level. An increase in loan production can lead to an increase in interest income, improving the NIM and consequently EPS. As long as deposits are so cheap, I remain positive.

As for the dividend, the sharp drop in the share price has brought the dividend yield to over 6%. At least for the moment, the dividend seems sustainable (payout ratio 80%), but a further collapse of EPS could change the situation. There is no reason to think so today, but we know how quickly the macroeconomic context can change due to the tariffs issue.

Another aspect I don't like about this dividend is that it is not growing.

Sure, the yield is very high, but there is no growth from 2022. When I invest in a dividend company, growth potential is one of the aspects I care about the most. There is a risk that by buying PPBI your dividend yield on cost will not increase over time.

Conclusion

PPBI is a sound bank, not only because it has low-cost deposits and good credit risk management, but also because it has a high CET1 (17.05%).

The ample liquidity available has a positive impact on capital ratios, but perhaps it is better to take more risks and invest this capital to generate an income. In fact, this is what the management is doing, given that the demand for loans is still weak. Between loan origination and purchases, the loan portfolio has the potential to grow at a mid-single digit rate in 2025, and with a low cost of deposits, I expect the NIM to improve.

Currently, its Tangible Book Value is priced well below its historical average, which implies a very high upside potential in case of mean reversion. As for the dividend, it is sustainable for the time being, but the lack of growth weighs negatively.

Thanks to its low Price/TBV per share, I believe that PPBI deserves a buy rating, but being a cyclical business, there can be sudden changes based on the evolution of the economic context. These days, we are reading a lot of news about a greater probability of facing a recession following the introduction of tariffs, which leads me to maintain a high level of caution with this type of investment. As long as the economy remains stable, I believe that PPBI has a good chance of a turnaround, but in the event of a recession there is not much it can do. Certainly, the fact that it manages credit risk well puts it in an advantageous position compared to its peers, but there is little it can do if the entire industry collapses.

data.title: Stocks Purr, Tariffs Roar
data.description:
Stocks crept higher today, looking like a house cat cautiously emerging after the vacuum cleaner has been stowed. Three straight intraday rebounds nearly erased Friday’s 2% S&P spanking.

Level Change 4/2/25 (%)

+0.6 Dow

+0.9 Nasdaq

+0.8 Nasdaq 100

+0.7 S&P 500

+1.6 S&P 400

+1.5 S&P 600

Today was mostly a waiting game ahead of President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement after the bell, the market hoping he wouldn’t deliver another populist piñata swing. Sky News floated the idea of a tiered approach: tariffs at 10%, 15%, and 20%, parceled out by country and industry like party favors no one wants.

Traders found solace in the forecast not being a flat 20% for all, like cheering that the flood only reached the first floor. Hope for a patchwork plan did little to reduce the haze surrounding Trump 2.0, prompting more talk of selling strength, or what passes for strength in a three-day shuffle vaguely resembling an ascent.

After hours, among the fragrant blooms of the Rose Garden, came thunder and tariffs.

Trump signed an executive order imposing 10% levies on all trading partners, and double-digit “reciprocal” ones on dozens of other countries deemed dastardly or insufficiently deferential.

The tallies read like a parody of a customs invoice: 20% on the European Union, 24% on Japan, and 26% on India. All told, more than 100 trading partners found themselves newly baptized in the gospel of economic retribution.

And they’d better get used to it. The administration said it’s in no mood to haggle, with Trump calling the move “kind” because it charges only half as much as it should. “We’re going to start being smart,” he said, “and we’re going to start being very wealthy again.”

To inaugurate the return to riches, S&P 500 futures slid 2%, erasing the three-day rebound that erased Friday’s sell-off. Chatter is simple: here come the countermeasures, and Trump’s economic plan really is a trade war, while China sprints ahead on AI and other fields that couldn’t care less about customs declarations. Our mandarin man says, “you slap tariffs, we write code.”

At least the hard vs. soft data outperformance persists.

March ADP private payrolls rose 155,000, well above the 120,000 consensus and up sharply from February’s upwardly revised 84,000. Wage growth rose 4.6% for those loyal enough to stay put, and 6.5% for those brazen enough to jump ship, both slightly cooler month-on-month.

Manufacturing was stronger than average, but construction slowed. Investors squinted at these tea leaves, trying to divine what Friday’s official employment report might hold.

ADP chief economist Nela Richardson dotted the “i” in dichotomy: “Despite policy uncertainty and downbeat consumers, the bottom line is this: The March topline number was a good one for the economy and employers of all sizes, if not necessarily all sectors.”

After the close, Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said progress toward the 2% inflation target has slowed down or possibly stalled altogether. She must have wandered through the Rose Garden en route to Princeton, where her words in academic mist emphasized the role of inflation expectations - bad, given “upside risks associated with announced and prospective policy changes.” She probably didn’t mean the renaming of that gulf off America’s southern coast.

Walmart (WMT +1.1%) has been trying to weather the tariff typhoon by pressuring Chinese suppliers to take one for the trading team. It negotiated so aggressively for lower prices last month it ruffled the robes of Beijing’s mandarins, but that didn’t slow it down.

In business, margins trump diplomacy. The risk of losing access to US consumers has some Chinese factory owners scouting other locations in Asia, to which Beijing thugs undoubtedly hissed, “Oh, we’ll show you other locations in Asia” - the kind where production stops permanently, along with everything else.

Then there’s Tesla (TSLA +5.3%), whose board must be quietly Googling “CEO succession protocol” as their CEO’s political activities crush sales. First quarter deliveries of 337,000 came in well below the 378,000 consensus, which prompted the following little dispatch from Politico: Elon Musk will soon be stepping back from his extracurricular role as Washington hatchet man. Apparently, he and Trump have mutually agreed that “it will soon be time for Musk to return to his businesses.” You don’t say.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway Brands: I Remain Negative Due To The Visible Headwinds
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Topgolf Callaway Brands (NYSE:MODG) in Nov’24, which I recommended a sell rating on due to my expectation that the near-term outlook is going to be weak, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I reiterate my sell rating for MODG as the macro conditions have gotten worse, and there are no signs of recovery/improvement in MODG's key operating metrics.

Investment thesis

Let me deal with the bigger picture. There has been an obvious pullback in discretionary consumer spending, and I think that is going to pose a real problem for MODG, much more than I thought previously—especially for the Topgolf segment, which is heavily dependent on consumers’ spending power.

We can see from McKinsey data that US consumers are pulling back across the majority of discretionary spending. And if combined with that other macro data such as the recent retail sales data—down 0.9% y/y in February—and consumer confidence dipping again in March to 106.7, it paints a very weak consumer spending environment. The sentiment at the Fed level has also shifted to a negative tone, wherein they have cut US GDP growth. I think there is a real likelihood of a recession happening, something Goldman has raised the odds of. If true, I strongly believe this backdrop creates a difficult environment for MODG to grow same-venue sales [SVS] or drive visit frequency among its users. This is an even tougher setup than when I last wrote about the business in November.

Same-Venue Sales Are Still Declining

In fact, the SVS trends are already pointing in the wrong direction. Blended SVS declined 8.5% y/y in 4Q24, and the weakness was across both the consumer and corporate segments—down 10% and 5%, respectively. Management also guided to another 10–13% y/y drop in 1Q25, which clearly shows that weaknesses have persisted with no immediate sight of a recovery. While some of the weakness in 1Q25 can be explained by fewer weekends in the quarter, I think this does not matter. The point is MODG simply hasn’t recovered the momentum it had in FY22 when SVS trended in the high-single-digit to low-teens percentage range. And even if the comp base gets easier through FY25 (1Q to 3Q saw accelerating SVS decline), any form of positive SVS growth is probably just optics (turned positive because of easy comps) unless it is of significant magnitude. The more probable outcome, I believe, is that SVS growth remains negative as the broader consumer spending environment stays weak.

Topgolf Expansion Plans Have Quietly Slowed

In a normalized environment, MODG could have just ramped up venue openings to drive consolidated revenue growth. However, I think this growth lever is not going to be effective, and MODG numbers show it—MODG only opened 7 new domestic venues in FY24, below the past three years average of 10. Management also expects to open just five new venues in 2025, a bigger step down from the average. Although management framed this as a strategic decision to focus on the productivity of existing venues, I have a different read on this. I think this reflects the state of the underlying demand. Put simply, the demand environment does not justify MODG opening 10 stores. It is good that management did not push through with opening stores for the sake of doing so, but this also shows that the runway for new unit growth may stay flat at 5 stores or less, which weakens one of the few levers MODG can pull to offset SVS declines.

Cost pressure

Finally, labor inflation is a significant overhang. Per the latest release, wage inflation grew ~4% y/y, trending above the broader inflation rate. With inflation being persistent at ~3% and no real signs of coming down, my view is that wage inflation will continue to stay where they are. While this is not unique to MODG, it is a big headwind for a business with a high fixed-cost base. Remember that the two biggest fixed costs to the MODG business model are labor and occupancy—they need these two to operate the venues.

At a time when demand is weak, it is going to be extremely difficult or near impossible, in my opinion, for MODG to raise prices to offset this cost pressure. As such, I think margin compression is likely to persist well into 2025.

Valuation

Valuation-wise, my view is the same. I don’t see a strong reason why MODG would see any upwards re-rating of its valuation multiple. Like I have expected, MODG forward EBITDA multiple has indeed stayed depressed at ~7+x since November, and I believe it would continue to bounce around this range. The worst-case scenario is that valuation dips to Covid lows of ~5x forward EBITDA. I wouldn’t put this in the “probable” range, but I wouldn’t entirely dismiss it if the US economy goes into a recession. The reasoning behind it is simple: a recession would heavily impact SVS growth, further limit unit growth, and cause EBITDA margins to take a dive because of operating leverage.

Risk

The main upside risk is a faster-than-expected recovery in consumer spending, which would not only support a rebound in MODG SVS but also drive a strong turnaround in sentiment. Additionally, any update on portfolio separation could re-rate the stock meaningfully. Lastly, if somehow MODG is able to reduce fixed costs to protect margins in this downturn, it would be viewed positively, potentially limiting the downside.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for MODG is still a sell rating. The macro environment is a lot worse than before, and MODG is evidently impacted by this. SVS trends continue to deteriorate, and unit growth is slowing. Meanwhile, rising cost pressure put earnings margins at further risk. Until there is an improvement in demand, I don’t see a reason to upgrade the stock’s rating.

data.title: Dillard's: Fundamentals Have Deteriorated (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Dillard’s (NYSE:DDS) was a hold rating because I was not convinced that the fundamentals have improved, especially the demand and margin outlook. True enough, the stock tanked after the recent results, which further validates my view that DDS is still facing a lot of challenges. I downgrade my rating from hold to sell as I believe the fundamentals have deteriorated, with the potential for more earnings pressure in the coming quarters.

Visible macro and micro weaknesses

While I have already pointed out the macro weakness as a headwind for DDS, since November (my last writeup for DDS), macro conditions have also turned more unfavorable. Consumer confidence has dropped by a lot, with only 16.3% of US consumers in March expecting their incomes to rise, down from 18.8% in February. Meanwhile, 15.5% expected their incomes to decline, up from 12.8% the prior month. These are obvious signs that point to a more cautious consumer mindset. And importantly, Trump’s tariffs have not even gone live yet, which I see as huge risks that it could result in a demand shock, further worsening the spending environment. These are particularly relevant for discretionary-focused retailers like DDS. This overall pullback in spending is also showing up directly in DDS numbers.

First of all, same-store sales [SSS] have declined for the 8th consecutive quarter, and the most concerning data point, in my view, is that gross profit dollars have declined for the 9th straight quarter in 4Q24, with 4Q24 marking the worst decline over the past two years. Even on a margin basis, gross margin fell 170 bps y/y to 34.9% in the quarter, and on a three-year stacked basis, it has now declined by 590 bps— worsening from a 365 bps stack decline in 3Q24. This tells us that the deterioration in fundamentals is accelerating, not stabilizing. While management did not break out the drivers in detail, my belief is that DDS is suffering heavily from increased markdown pressure and softer full-price sales, both of which stemmed from poor underlying demand (the SSS decline is compelling proof).

This decline in gross profit dollars is especially problematic for multiple reasons. First, as I have mentioned before, it creates negative operating leverage, which reduces earnings power even if management is able to manage SG&A. Second, it pushes out the timeline for DDS to see any form of margin normalization. This puts a limit on the stock sentiment. And third, it suggests that DDS is not able to exert any form of pricing power. Let me put this from another perspective. If DDS is not able to see leverage on meaningful full-price sell-through in the peak holiday period like in 4Q to support gross profit dollar growth, it is very unlikely DDS would see any traction in the next three quarters.

The worst thing is that I don’t think we have seen the end of this gross profit decline. I now have a stronger view that this trend will continue for the foreseeable future. It is now apparent to me that DDS made a mistake to load up on inventories in 3Q24 and 4Q24 (inventory grew 3.3% and 7.1%, respectively). Clearly, the holiday season demand was not strong enough, as evident from the SSS performance, and this has resulted in a significant spread between sales and inventory growth. For reference, 4Q24 total sales to inventory growth delta was a whopping 12.7% (inventory growth minus sales growth). This is the widest spread since the pandemic in 2Q20 and is much worse than the ~700bps spread seen in 3Q24. Given that consumer demand is unlikely to see a sharp rebound anytime soon, I see a risk for more markdown in the upcoming quarters.

Lastly, the weakness in DDS SSS is not something that can be easily fixed even if DDS wants to. If we look across all the categories, all of them saw negative growth except for cosmetics, which is just a small portion of overall sales. The issue is when multiple core categories are weakening at the same time; DDS would need to spread its resources across all categories to bump things up. This is unlike just one or two categories falling short, and management could simply allocate more resources over the short term to ramp up marketing or product newness. As such, I think there is a very high hurdle for DDS to drive SSS recovery unless the overall economy recovers.

Valuation

While DDS valuation has derated from 16x to ~14x today, I think there is still a lot more room for multiples to go down. DDS is still trading in line with its historical average of ~14x, which I think is not justified given the visible weakness ahead—gross profit should continue to decline, which impacts earnings directly due to operating leverage. The low end of DDS trading bend is ~8.5x over the past 10 years, and if we assume that is the floor, DDS valuation could still go down by ~40%.

Risk

If consumer sentiment improves meaningfully or discretionary spending rebounds in FY25, DDS will benefit heavily from this as it is able to achieve better full-price sell-through that will drive up margins. Although I don’t think this is likely, it is also an upside risk that if DDS is able to implement an across-the-board marketing/pricing strategy that works without adding more pressure on margins, it would drive a strong sentiment around the stock—that DDS has found the solution to navigate this tricky backdrop.

Conclusion

Putting it all together, I think the bear case on DDS is now even more compelling than it was in late 2024. Gross profit dollars have now declined for nine straight quarters, and I expect this trend to continue for the next couple of quarters because inventories are too high relative to sales, which should result in more markdowns. While there are some upside risks, I believe the near-term earnings quality and sentiment setup are not great. The stock still trades in line with its historical average despite the earnings weakness. As such, I see potential for more downside.

data.title: Baidu: Significant Value At A Low Price
data.description:
Shares of Baidu (NASDAQ:BIDU) have consolidated since October 2024, which is when initial excitement about Beijing's stimulus package started to fizzle out. The valuation draw-down creates a new investment opportunity for investors. However, that want to add a highly profitable Chinese large-cap to their growth portfolios. Baidu beat fourth-quarter EPS estimates last month and continued to return a lot of money to shareholders via stock buybacks. I see Baidu as significantly underpriced, given its high profitability and expected earnings growth. I also expect the company to maintain its lead in the online search market and continue to buy back its shares, which obviously makes a lot of sense considering that Baidu is priced at a 12.3% earnings yield.

Previous rating

I recommended shares of Baidu as a strong buy in my last work on the Chinese company because I saw a strong value proposition in the search market and potential for a post-pandemic recovery that was set to benefit all major Chinese companies: Too Cheap To Ignore. Although Baidu experienced top line pressure in Q4’24, the company can capitalize on its strong market position in the online marketing business and has potential to grow its earnings long term if it can maintain its lead over the competition. The tech company also remains an attractive capital return play for investors that like Baidu's stock buybacks at a time when shares are extraordinarily cheap and undervalued.

Strong value proposition based off of profitability in its core market

Baidu owns China’s largest search engine which ensures that the company experiences a steady stream of recurring advertising-related marketing income. Baidu generated 34.1B Chinese Yuan ($4.7B) in revenue in the fourth fiscal quarter, showing a 2% top decline relative to the corresponding period a year earlier. While Baidu's adjusted earnings declined 13% year-over-year to 6.7B Chinese Yuan ($919M) in Q4'24, the digital marketing platform remained widely profitable and retains massive potential to execute on stock buybacks in FY 2025.

What I like about Baidu specifically is that the company, despite growing pressure from Bing in the Chinese search engine market, is still very much dominating the Search engine market. As per Statcounter, Baidu has a market share in the Chinese Search engine market of 51.5% in February 2025. While Bing has made inroads in the Chinese market in the last year (its market share stood at 30.7% in February 2025), Baidu still has a massive lead of approximately 21 PP over its second-largest online rival. This puts Baidu into a strong position for sustainable, long-term earnings growth and gives the company also considerable power to finance new stock buybacks.

Baidu is, despite its muted top line performance, a highly profitable enterprise that can hold its ground when compared to rivals in the large-cap tech space. While it doesn't have the highest gross profits compared to Alibaba or Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), Baidu has a very strong gross margin profile, due to a highly profitable digital advertising core business. In Q4'24, Baidu's gross margin totaled 47.2%, making it the most profitable large-cap in the industry group after Tencent.

Stock buybacks are one way for the technology company to return more cash to shareholders and Baidu is buying back hundreds of millions of dollars worth of its own shares quarterly. In Q4'24, Baidu repurchased $356M of its own shares, bringing the stock buyback total in FY 2024 to more than $1.0B. Since the tech company announced its $5.0B stock buyback authorization in FY 2023, Baidu has repurchased approximately $1.7B (34% of its authorization) worth of its own shares. Going forward, I expect Baidu to continue to invest in AI products that could boost its core digital marketing performance, but also return more cash to shareholders through continual share repurchases in the open market.

Baidu is consistently free cash flow-profitable, although its FCF has been proven to be volatile from quarter to quarter. The tech company achieved an average quarterly FCF of $925M in the last five years...

Baidu’s valuation

Baidu is one of the cheapest large-cap companies in the market and shares are a bargain from a valuation point of view, in my opinion. Baidu is currently valued at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.2X, which implies an earnings yield of 12.3%. For comparison, Alibaba is priced at a forward price-to-earnings ratio, also on a FY 2026 forward earnings basis, of 11.6X which implies an earnings yield of 8.6%.

I like Alibaba as the e-Commerce company has recently seen a slight top line growth acceleration and continues to be very profitable on a free cash flow basis. In fact, I believe that Alibaba's massive e-Commerce business, known as the Taobao and Tmall Group, as well as accelerating growth in Cloud, translate into $20B annual free cash flow potential: Why I Am More Bullish Than Ever.

Baidu could easily be valued at 11-12X forward earnings, in my opinion, especially if the Search engine defends its leading position in the online search and advertising market. An 11-12X P/E ratio implies a fair value in a range of $124-135 per-share. This fair value range could change going forward based off of Baidu's execution in its core segment (maintaining its market share lead over Bing) as well as its plans with regard to its stock buybacks.

Risks with Baidu

Although Baidu has a strong market position in online advertising, there are other search engines that have recently made inroads in the online digital advertising market, especially Bing. Bing's rise could potentially erode Baidu's market position and lead to pressure on its core segment. What would change my mind about Baidu is if the technology company were to see an erosion in its core market position. Additionally, I would see a decrease in the company's stock buybacks and declining gross margins as a negative for my positive stock rating.

Final thoughts

Baidu reported decent earnings for its fourth-quarter last month, beat estimates and once again underlined that it is riding high on its core digital marketing business. The most attractive feature of an investment in Baidu is the company’s low valuation, in my opinion, which implies a double-digit earnings yield of 12.3%. With Baidu still owning China's largest search engine, and dominating the market (and having a material lead over Bing), I believe the company has potential for consistent earnings growth. Stock buybacks are also a great way for Baidu to unlock value for shareholders given that shares are trading at such a low price-to-earnings ratio.
